stmt: ∀ {Γ : List Tp} {A : Tp}, proveAux (list_degree Γ + tp_degree A) Γ A ↔ (Γ ⇒ A)
priority_rationale: Old proof-search equivalence work is far from the present residual-graph frontier.

stmt: Introduce a proof predicate for singleton sequents that permits only ax, ldiv_r, and rdiv_r, and prove: for all A C : Tp, [A] ⇒ C if and only if there exists such a left-rule-free derivation of [A] ⇒ C.
priority_rationale: Historic singleton-normalization packaging no longer drives the current theory summary.

stmt: Introduce an inductive proof predicate for sequents from atom-only contexts using only `ax`, `ldiv_r`, and `rdiv_r`, and prove: for all `{Γ : List Tp} {A : Tp}`, `(∀ x ∈ Γ, is_atom x) → ((Γ ⇒ A) ↔ AtomOnlyNormalDerivation Γ A)`.
priority_rationale: Atom-only derivation encoding has been superseded by later residual and candidate-tree results.

stmt: Define what it means for a type and a context to lie entirely in the left-only fragment or entirely in the right-only fragment, and prove: if every formula in `Γ` lies in the left-only fragment, then `(Γ ⇒ # s)` in the full product-free calculus iff the corresponding translated context derives `# s` in the left-only calculus; likewise for the right-only fragment.
priority_rationale: One-sided fragment translation is already represented by stronger conservativity theorems.

stmt: Prove a mixed-handed boundary theorem for the recursive support invariant: if every formula in Γ lies entirely in the left-only fragment or entirely in the right-only fragment, then for every atom s, support_ok Γ (# s) is equivalent to the naive support condition ∃ B ∈ Γ, occurs_atom s B; moreover, any failure of this flat condition in the full calculus requires genuinely mixed-handed context formulas.
priority_rationale: Mixed-handed support boundary work is no longer central after exact handed-support results.

stmt: Define what it means for an arbitrary type to lie entirely in the left-only fragment or entirely in the right-only fragment, and prove: if every formula in Γ and A is left-only, then Γ ⇒ A in the full product-free calculus iff the corresponding translated sequent is derivable in the left-only calculus; dually, if every formula in Γ and A is right-only, then full derivability is equivalent to derivability in the right-only calculus.
priority_rationale: Superseded by established one-sided conservativity and outside the active frontier.

stmt: Define the collection of formulas occurring anywhere in a candidate tree, and prove a global subformula property: whenever Γ ⇒ A, there exists a candidate tree for Γ and A in which every intermediate formula is a subformula of some formula in Γ or of A.
priority_rationale: Global subformula packaging was a precursor, but residual-state control now carries the structural load.

stmt: Construct the finite state graph generated by all subformula-bounded candidate-tree decompositions of a fixed sequent, and prove that Γ ⇒ A holds exactly when the root state (Γ, A) is accepted by that graph.
priority_rationale: Finite candidate-tree graph recognition has been overtaken by the verified residual-graph recognizer.

stmt: Prove that if every formula in Γ and A lies entirely in the left-only fragment, then every global proof-search decomposition of Γ ⇒ A stays entirely in the left-only fragment; dually, if every formula lies in the right-only fragment, every such decomposition stays entirely in the right-only fragment.
priority_rationale: Fragment closure under decomposition is old support material, not a current bottleneck.

stmt: Prove a cross-fragment rigidity theorem: if every formula in Γ is left-only and A is right-only, then Γ ⇒ A implies A is atomic; dually, if every formula in Γ is right-only and A is left-only, then Γ ⇒ A implies A is atomic.
priority_rationale: Cross-fragment rigidity is structurally interesting but disconnected from the active exact-reachability program.

stmt: Define intrinsic candidate-tree notions for the left-only and right-only calculi, and prove that on one-sided sequents the translation to the full calculus induces a size-preserving bijection between fragment candidate trees and full candidate trees.
priority_rationale: Fragment candidate-tree bijections are peripheral to the present executable residual agenda.

stmt: Define a subformula relation on `Tp`, and prove that whenever `residualAtomicState Γ A = some (Δ, s)` and `AtomicCandidateTree Δ s`, every formula appearing anywhere in that atomic search tree lies in the subformula closure of the original sequent `(Γ, A)`.
priority_rationale: Residual atomic-tree subformula control is already absorbed by stronger reachable invariants.

stmt: If `residualAtomicState Γ A = some (Δ, s)` and `AtomicResidualGraphAccepts (Δ, s)`, then every state visited along any accepting run from `(Δ, s)` has the form `(Δ', s)` with every formula in `Δ'` a subformula of some formula in `Γ` or of `A`.
priority_rationale: Per-run subformula invariance is weaker than the verified all-reachable invariant.

stmt: There exist Γ A Δ s and q such that residualAtomicState Γ A = some (Δ, s), q.2 = s, and every formula in q.1 is a subformula of some formula in Γ ++ [A], but q is not reachable from (Δ, s).
summary_delta: The new theorem gives a necessary condition for reachability. Determining whether that condition is strictly weaker than actual reachability would clarify the boundary of the residual-control story: if strict, then subformula-bounded slices are only over-approximations and later automata constructions will need a sharper reachability criterion, not just the subformula invariant.
priority_rationale: Strictness of the subformula bound has already been explored and no longer changes the summary.

stmt: Define a self-contained branching residual automaton on atomic states: from `(Γ, s)`, each division candidate yields the continuation state together with the residual state obtained by residualizing its argument premise. Prove that `Γ ⇒ A` iff the initial state returned by `residualAtomicState Γ A` is accepted by this internal branching automaton.
priority_rationale: Interesting abstraction, but the current exact-graph program has moved past this older automaton setup.

stmt: There is a finite branching automaton built only from subformulas of Γ ++ [A] such that, whenever residualAtomicState Γ A = some (Δ, s), acceptance from (Δ, s) in the residual graph is equivalent to acceptance in that finite automaton.
summary_delta: This turns the new subformula control into a closed finite recognizer, which is exactly the next outward-looking abstraction suggested by the current frontier. It is stronger than a root-only bound and materially advances the program of internalizing residual recognition without relying on external graph machinery.
priority_rationale: A true finite internal automaton would reconnect the work to decidability and grammar themes, but it is not the nearest bottleneck.

stmt: For any Γ A Δ s, if residualAtomicState Γ A = some (Δ, s), then the set of residual-graph states reachable from (Δ, s) is finite.
summary_delta: Once residual states are known to stay within subformulas of the original sequent, a genuine theory-level consequence is finiteness of the reachable region. That is a reusable metatheorem for recognition and decidability, and it advances the current bottleneck more directly than additional witness-packaging lemmas.
priority_rationale: Already subsumed by the verified finite reachable-region theorem.

stmt: For any finite source list Σ and states p q, if every formula in p.1 is a subformula of some formula in Σ and AtomicResidualGraphStep p q, then q.2 = p.2 and every formula in q.1 is a subformula of some formula in Σ.
summary_delta: The solved theorem was proved by propagating a one-step closure property through reachability. Isolating that closure as its own theorem turns the current argument into a reusable invariant principle, which is the right abstraction for later finiteness and internal-automaton results rather than redoing reachability inductions from a specific residual root.
priority_rationale: Already subsumed by the verified one-step subformula-closure theorem.

stmt: ∀ (Γ Δ : List Tp) (A : Tp) (s : String), residualAtomicState Γ A = some (Δ, s) → ((Γ ⇒ A) ↔ Relation.ReflTransGen AtomicResidualGraphStep (Δ, s) ([# s], s))
priority_rationale: This root-to-base formulation is effectively subsumed by current graph-recognition and base-reachability theorems.

stmt: For every finite list src and atom s, the class of states p such that p.2 = s and every formula B in p.1 is a subformula of some formula C in src is finite.
summary_delta: This is the ambient finite-control theorem behind reachable-region finiteness. Combined with the solved one-step preservation result and the existing all-reachable invariant, it turns residual dynamics into a genuinely finite state space rather than a merely bounded invariant.
priority_rationale: Already refuted by the verified same-atom subformula infinitude counterexample.

stmt: There exist Γ, A, Δ, s, and a state p such that residualAtomicState Γ A = some (Δ, s), p.2 = s, every formula in p.1 is a subformula of some formula in Γ ++ [A], but p is not reachable from (Δ, s) by residual-graph steps.
summary_delta: This would sharply separate the current necessary same-atom subformula bound from true reachability. Resolving that boundary is not peripheral: it determines whether the bounded state space is already exact or only an overapproximation that still needs a structural reachability criterion.
priority_rationale: More ambient-slice separation evidence is overexplored and no longer summary-changing.

stmt: If residualAtomicState Γ A = some (Δ, s), then acceptance from (Δ, s) is unchanged when the residual graph is restricted to states p with p.2 = s and with every formula in p.1 a subformula of some formula in Γ ++ [A].
summary_delta: This is the key internalization step from the global residual graph to an input-determined bounded subgraph. It directly advances the agenda of replacing external residual-graph acceptance by an exact finite internal recognizer.
priority_rationale: Ambient-slice restriction is less central than exact reachable computation and looks close to existing invariants.

stmt: ∀ (src : List Tp) (s : String) (N : Nat), Set.Finite { p : AtomicResidualState | p.2 = s ∧ p.1.length ≤ N ∧ ∀ B ∈ p.1, ∃ C ∈ src, Subformula B C }
summary_delta: This is the clean repair suggested by the logs and by the counterexample: the obstruction is unbounded list multiplicity, not subformula variety. Proving finiteness under a uniform length bound would give a reusable ambient finite-slice lemma that immediately becomes relevant if later residual arguments can bound context length, and it remains meaningfully distinct from the existing degree-bounded open problem.
priority_rationale: Already subsumed by the verified degree-bounded slice finiteness theorem.

stmt: ∀ (Γ Δ : List Tp) (A : Tp) (s : String), residualAtomicState Γ A = some (Δ, s) → ((Γ ⇒ A) ↔ ∃ n : Nat, AtomicBranchLength Δ s n ∧ n ≤ list_degree Δ)
priority_rationale: This branch-length packaging belongs to an older proof-search route rather than the current frontier.

stmt: ∀ (Γ Δ : List Tp) (A : Tp) (s : String), residualAtomicState Γ A = some (Δ, s) → ((∀ p : AtomicResidualState, p.2 = s → list_degree p.1 ≤ list_degree Δ → (∀ B ∈ p.1, ∃ C ∈ Γ ++ [A], Subformula B C) → Relation.ReflTransGen AtomicResidualGraphStep (Δ, s) p) ↔ ∃ q, Relation.ReflTransGen AtomicResidualGraphStep (Δ, s) q ∧ AtomicResidualGraphStep q q)
summary_delta: The counterexample was witnessed by a dead residual root with no outgoing step, suggesting a structural explanation for exactness failure. This candidate asks whether bounded-slice exactness breaks exactly when reachability gets trapped before covering the whole ambient slice. That would convert the new existential separation into a reusable criterion rather than another isolated example.
priority_rationale: An overfitted exactness criterion on an already overexplored ambient slice.

stmt: ∀ (Γ Δ : List Tp) (A : Tp) (s : String), residualAtomicState Γ A = some (Δ, s) → Set.Finite {p : AtomicResidualState | p.2 = s ∧ list_degree p.1 ≤ list_degree Δ ∧ (∀ B ∈ p.1, ∃ C ∈ Γ ++ [A], Subformula B C)} ∧ ¬((∀ p : AtomicResidualState, p.2 = s → list_degree p.1 ≤ list_degree Δ → (∀ B ∈ p.1, ∃ C ∈ Γ ++ [A], Subformula B C) → Relation.ReflTransGen AtomicResidualGraphStep (Δ, s) p))
summary_delta: This packages the emerging global lesson suggested by the solved example: finite control from degree bounds is available, but exactness of the most obvious bounded ambient slice can still fail. Establishing that coexistence cleanly would sharpen the theory’s current bottleneck and justify shifting effort from ambient finiteness toward exact bounded internalization.
priority_rationale: It repackages a boundary lesson already known, but does not advance exact computation.

stmt: ∀ (src : List Tp) (s : String), Set.Finite { p : AtomicResidualState | p.2 = s ∧ ∀ B ∈ p.1, ∃ C ∈ src, Subformula B C } ↔ src = []
summary_delta: The verified counterexample shows the ambient same-atom subformula slice blows up by arbitrary repetition as soon as any source formula is available. An exact iff criterion would sharply settle that this route cannot yield finite control except in the trivial empty-source case, which is valuable global guidance for the residual-control program.
priority_rationale: This settles an ambient negative boundary but adds little reusable bridge value now.

stmt: ∀ n : Nat, ∃ (Γ Δ : List Tp) (A : Tp) (s : String), residualAtomicState Γ A = some (Δ, s) ∧ ∃ S : Finset AtomicResidualState, S.card ≥ n ∧ (∀ p ∈ S, p.2 = s ∧ list_degree p.1 ≤ list_degree Δ ∧ (∀ B ∈ p.1, ∃ C ∈ Γ ++ [A], Subformula B C) ∧ ¬ Relation.ReflTransGen AtomicResidualGraphStep (Δ, s) p)
summary_delta: The solved statement shows the bounded same-atom subformula slice can miss exact reachability even when degree is bounded. A natural next boundary question is whether this failure is merely sporadic or can be made arbitrarily large. Proving unbounded separation would sharply rule out this ambient slice as a near-exact finite machine model.
priority_rationale: An unbounded negative separation family is anti-goal territory without new constructive leverage.

stmt: ∀ (Γ : List Tp) (A : Tp) (Δ : List Tp) (s : String), residualAtomicState Γ A = some (Δ, s) → ∃ S : Finset AtomicResidualState, (∀ p ∈ S, p.2 = s ∧ list_degree p.1 ≤ list_degree Δ ∧ (∀ B ∈ p.1, ∃ C ∈ Γ ++ [A], Subformula B C)) ∧ (∀ p, Relation.ReflTransGen AtomicResidualGraphStep (Δ, s) p → p ∈ S)
summary_delta: The solved theorem turns the ambient degree-bounded same-atom subformula slice into a finite container. Packaging every exact reachable set inside an explicit finite state set would bridge ambient finiteness to constructive internal recognition without assuming the whole slice is exact.
priority_rationale: Exact reachable finsets are already available, so this container theorem adds little.

stmt: ∀ (Γ : List Tp) (A : Tp) (Δ : List Tp) (s : String), residualAtomicState Γ A = some (Δ, s) → ∃ n : Nat, ∀ p, Relation.ReflTransGen AtomicResidualGraphStep (Δ, s) p → list_degree p.1 ≤ n
summary_delta: This isolates the exact boundedness principle actually enjoyed by genuine reachable states. It is weaker and cleaner than an exact recursion theorem, but stronger than the just-solved ambient finiteness lemma because it extracts a root-specific degree bound from exact reachability itself.
priority_rationale: A root-specific degree bound is already implicit in strict degree descent from the root.

stmt: ∀ (Γ : List Tp) (A : Tp) (Δ : List Tp) (s : String), residualAtomicState Γ A = some (Δ, s) → Set.Finite { p : AtomicResidualState | p.2 = s ∧ list_degree p.1 ≤ list_degree Δ ∧ (∀ B ∈ p.1, ∃ C ∈ Γ ++ [A], Subformula B C) ∧ Relation.ReflTransGen AtomicResidualGraphStep (Δ, s) p }
summary_delta: The current theorem gives finiteness of the whole bounded ambient slice, but the theory still lacks a clean exact finite-state object tied to one root. This candidate isolates the exact reachable portion of that slice as a finite set, making the separation between ambient degree control and genuine reachability explicit and reusable.
priority_rationale: Exact reachable-set finiteness is already verified, so this is mostly bookkeeping.

stmt: For every `src : List Tp`, the set `{ B : Tp | ∃ C ∈ src, Subformula B C }` is finite.
summary_delta: This is the finite-alphabet bridge behind the solved criterion. Making subformula support finiteness explicit would give later exact internalization arguments a clean reusable entry point, instead of repeatedly hiding this step inside more specialized bounded-slice lemmas.
priority_rationale: Finite subformula alphabet control is useful, but too far below the current frontier.

stmt: For every finite `T : Set Tp` and `S : Set (List Tp)`, if `S ⊆ { Γ : List Tp | ∀ B ∈ Γ, B ∈ T }`, then `S.Finite ↔ ∃ N : Nat, ∀ Γ ∈ S, list_degree Γ ≤ N`.
summary_delta: This isolates the combinatorial core of the solved theorem from the residual-state packaging. It would make `list_degree` the exact finiteness control parameter for any finite-alphabet family of contexts, which is a reusable reduction for later bounded-state and backward-recursion constructions.
priority_rationale: Reusable combinatorial packaging, but too detached from the main bottlenecks.

stmt: ∀ (Γ Δ : List Tp) (A : Tp) (s : String), residualAtomicState Γ A = some (Δ, s) → every finite residual path starting at (Δ, s) has length at most list_degree Δ.
summary_delta: This sharpens reachable-set finiteness to a uniform height bound on residual exploration. That gives a direct, input-determined search cap for exact internal recognition and explains the global shape of the reachable region more clearly than finiteness alone.
priority_rationale: Uniform path-height control would help saturation bounds, though it is weaker than exact distance.

stmt: For any finite sequence of `n` residual-graph steps from `p` to `q`, if every formula in `p.1` is a subformula of some formula in `src`, then `q.2 = p.2`, every formula in `q.1` is still a subformula of some formula in `src`, and `list_degree q.1 + n ≤ list_degree p.1`.
summary_delta: This is the sharp multi-step abstraction exposed by the solved one-step theorem. It upgrades local descent to a reusable rank statement, yielding uniform path-height control and a degree-layered induction principle for later bounded-machine arguments.
priority_rationale: This reusable multi-step rank statement is a strong route to exact length and saturation bounds.

stmt: For every `src`, `s`, and `N`, the accepting states inside the bounded slice `{ p : AtomicResidualState | p.2 = s ∧ list_degree p.1 ≤ N ∧ (∀ B ∈ p.1, ∃ C ∈ src, Subformula B C) }` form the unique least subset `G` of that slice such that `([# s], s) ∈ G` and `p ∈ G ↔ p = ([# s], s) ∨ ∃ q ∈ G, AtomicResidualGraphStep p q`.
summary_delta: The solved theorem gives only an existential recursive predicate on the slice. Upgrading that to a unique least internal object would turn acceptance into a canonical backward fixed-point characterization, which is exactly the missing bridge from well-founded descent to exact internal recognition.
priority_rationale: Canonical fixed-point packaging on ambient slices is less valuable than exact reachable-region algorithms.

stmt: For every p : AtomicResidualState, if AtomicResidualGraphAccepts p, then there is a finite AtomicResidualGraphStep path from p to ([# (p.2)], p.2) whose length is at most list_degree p.1 - 1.
summary_delta: This turns the solved local existential recursion into a quantitative normalization theorem that uses list_degree as the exact recursion parameter. It is stronger than bare reachability and would be directly reusable for exact termination bounds and internal dynamic-programming arguments.
priority_rationale: Quantitative normalization to the base state would materially sharpen the current acceptance picture.

stmt: For every p : AtomicResidualState, there exists q such that Relation.ReflTransGen AtomicResidualGraphStep p q and there is no r with AtomicResidualGraphStep q r.
summary_delta: This promotes well-foundedness from an abstract accessibility fact to an explicit normalization theorem: every residual exploration reaches a terminal state. That gives a concrete endpoint structure for later exact reachability and decision arguments.
priority_rationale: Terminal-state existence is structurally clean but no longer the main missing bridge.

stmt: For every `p : AtomicResidualState`, if `R(p) := { q : AtomicResidualState | Relation.ReflTransGen AtomicResidualGraphStep p q }`, then the subset `{ q ∈ R(p) | AtomicResidualGraphAccepts q }` is the unique least subset `G ⊆ R(p)` such that `([# (p.2)], p.2) ∈ R(p) → ([# (p.2)], p.2) ∈ G`, and whenever `q ∈ R(p)`, `AtomicResidualGraphStep q r`, and `r ∈ G`, then `q ∈ G`.
summary_delta: The solved theorem identifies acceptance with exact reachability to the distinguished base state. The strongest next step is to turn that into a canonical predecessor-closed object on the exact reachable region itself, which is the missing bridge to later finite recognizers and least-fixed-point packaging without falling back to ambient slice overapproximations.
priority_rationale: Near-duplicate of the existing least reachable-subset characterization.

stmt: For every `Γ`, `A`, and `p` with `residualAtomicState Γ A = some p`, the exact reachable region from `p` carries a unique Boolean labeling of its states such that a reachable state is labeled `true` exactly when it is `([# (p.2)], p.2)` or has a residual step to another reachable state labeled `true`.
summary_delta: The solved theorem gives existence of such a labeling, but the main missing bridge is canonicity. Uniqueness turns the witness into the exact reachable-region recognizer that the current theory still lacks, and it is stronger than merely packaging acceptance as a least subset.
priority_rationale: Uniqueness of reachable Boolean labeling is already verified.

stmt: For every root state p and reachable states q r from p, if `Relation.ReflTransGen AtomicResidualGraphStep q r` and `Relation.ReflTransGen AtomicResidualGraphStep r q`, then `q = r`.
summary_delta: The proof of finiteness used strict degree descent along steps, which strongly suggests an explicit acyclicity theorem for the exact reachable region. Making that DAG structure explicit is not peripheral: it is the clean structural bridge needed for reverse recursion, uniqueness of terminal behavior, and canonical recognition on reachable states.
priority_rationale: Acyclicity is already effectively present via strict degree descent and topological-order theorems.

stmt: For every `Γ`, `A`, and `p` with `residualAtomicState Γ A = some p`, the states reachable from `p` admit a finite ordering in which every reachable step `AtomicResidualGraphStep q r` places `r` strictly earlier than `q`.
summary_delta: Degree descent is currently used only implicitly inside proofs. Making the reachable region into an explicit finite DAG/topological order would provide a reusable recursion principle and directly support later dynamic-programming and uniqueness results on the exact graph.
priority_rationale: A topological ordering is already essentially available from the current reachable-region results.

stmt: For every p q : AtomicResidualState, if Relation.ReflTransGen AtomicResidualGraphStep p q, then AtomicResidualGraphAccepts q ↔ Relation.ReflTransGen AtomicResidualGraphStep q ([# (p.2)], p.2).
summary_delta: The solved result identifies acceptance with the unique backward-propagated Boolean labeling on the exact reachable region. The next structural step is to collapse that recursion to a pure graph criterion: acceptance should be exactly reachability of the distinguished base sink from q. This is shorter, more reusable, and would clarify what the canonical labeling is actually computing.
priority_rationale: This graph criterion is already subsumed by acceptance-as-base-reachability.

stmt: For every root state p and every state q reachable from p, `AtomicResidualGraphAccepts q` iff every terminal state r reachable from q is equal to `([# (p.2)], p.2)`.
summary_delta: The solved result turned acceptance into a least predecessor-closed subset on a finite reachable region. This candidate sharpens that into a sink criterion on the finite residual DAG, explaining rejection by the presence of a non-base dead end and directly advancing the terminal-state bottleneck.
priority_rationale: Universal-terminal acceptance is weakened by mixed-terminal evidence and is not the right summary.

stmt: For all atomic residual states `p q`, if `AtomicResidualGraphStep p q` then `q.1.length < p.1.length`.
summary_delta: The counterexample works because every step needs a genuinely nonempty argument subcontext, so context length is the first universal monotone, not atomhood. This gives a simpler structural ranking of the reachable DAG, immediately explains why singleton states are terminal, and could support a more constructive reverse-sweep packaging than the current degree-only view.
priority_rationale: A length ranking would be nice, but it is off the main proof path and may be brittle.

stmt: For every `N : Nat`, there exists an atomic residual state `p` such that `list_degree p.1 ≥ N`, `¬ ∃ q, AtomicResidualGraphStep p q`, and `¬ AtomicResidualGraphAccepts p`.
summary_delta: The counterexample suggests that nonaccepting dead ends are not an isolated small-shape pathology. An unbounded-family theorem would show that rejecting terminal states cannot be handled by a tiny case split, which is important boundary information before investing further in sink summaries or terminal-state taxonomies.
priority_rationale: Another negative family about rejecting terminals adds boundary texture, not frontier change.

stmt: ∀ p : AtomicResidualState, (¬ ∃ q : AtomicResidualState, AtomicResidualGraphStep p q) → (AtomicResidualGraphAccepts p ↔ p = ([# (p.2)], p.2))
summary_delta: This sharply separates the unique accepting sink from nonaccepting dead ends. That gives the precise base case needed for any exact recursive acceptance procedure and explains why terminal ambient states can still fail to be genuinely accepting.
priority_rationale: Already subsumed by the verified terminal-acceptance classification.

stmt: For every p : AtomicResidualState, there exists a nodup list L whose elements are exactly the states q with Relation.ReflTransGen AtomicResidualGraphStep p q, and such that whenever AtomicResidualGraphStep q r with q,r in L, the position of r in L is strictly earlier than the position of q.
summary_delta: This packages the currently implicit degree-descent machinery into an explicit topological object on the exact reachable region. It is the clean structural bridge from finiteness to reverse recursion, and it advances the recognizer agenda more directly than a bare acyclicity statement.
priority_rationale: Explicit list packaging of the reachable DAG is useful, but weaker than current order theorems.

stmt: The universal sink criterion fails: there exist p q r : AtomicResidualState such that Relation.ReflTransGen AtomicResidualGraphStep p q, AtomicResidualGraphAccepts q, Relation.ReflTransGen AtomicResidualGraphStep q r, ¬ ∃ s, AtomicResidualGraphStep r s, and r ≠ ([# (p.2)], p.2).
summary_delta: The current theorem propagates truth by an existential successor rule, so it suggests that acceptance may depend on the existence of one successful branch rather than on all terminal descendants. Proving or refuting that stronger universal-sink picture is important before investing further in sink-based summaries; this separation would rule out a misleading direction and sharpen the global shape of the exact reachable DAG theory.
priority_rationale: The mixed-terminal failure is already witnessed by verified counterexample evidence.

stmt: For every `p q : AtomicResidualState`, if `Relation.ReflTransGen AtomicResidualGraphStep p q` and `¬ ∃ r, AtomicResidualGraphStep q r`, then `AtomicResidualGraphAccepts q ↔ q = ([# (p.2)], p.2)`.
summary_delta: This extracts a sharp sink classification from the canonical labeling: the distinguished base state is the only accepting terminal state in the exact reachable region. That is a reusable structural boundary result, and it is the accepting-side companion to the existing push toward nonaccepting terminal-obstruction theorems.
priority_rationale: Already covered by the terminal singleton-base classification.

stmt: For all `q r : AtomicResidualState`, if `Relation.ReflTransGen AtomicResidualGraphStep q r` and `AtomicResidualGraphAccepts r`, then `AtomicResidualGraphAccepts q`.
summary_delta: This isolates the key backward-closure mechanism used in the resolved proof into a reusable reachability principle. It is theory-level rather than proof-local, and it would streamline later terminal-frontier and reverse-sweep arguments by letting acceptance move cleanly backward along the reachable residual DAG.
priority_rationale: Backward closure is already built into the existing acceptance characterizations.

stmt: For every `r : AtomicResidualState`, if `¬ ∃ s : AtomicResidualState, AtomicResidualGraphStep r s`, then `AtomicResidualGraphAccepts r ↔ r = ([# (r.2)], r.2)`.
summary_delta: This would classify accepting terminal states exactly, turning terminal acceptance from a reachability condition into a concrete rigidity statement about sink shape. That is a clean global description of the terminal frontier and would make later frontier theorems substantially more transparent and reusable.
priority_rationale: Already exactly verified by the terminal acceptance theorem.

stmt: For every `p : AtomicResidualState`, if `L` is the degree-sorted topological list of the states reachable from `p`, then the concrete one-pass reverse scan along `L` that writes `true` at `q` exactly when `q = ([# (p.2)], p.2)` or some already-scanned successor of `q` is `true` produces a Boolean table agreeing pointwise with `AtomicResidualGraphAccepts` on the reachable region.
summary_delta: The solved result only shows that any labeling satisfying the local recursion is unique; it does not yet identify an actual executable sweep with that labeling. This candidate is the direct algorithmic bridge from abstract canonicity to an exact recognizer on the reachable DAG, while staying narrower than full all-orders invariance.
priority_rationale: An explicit reverse scan on the canonical order would give a concrete algorithm, albeit presentation-specific.

stmt: ∃ q r t : AtomicResidualState, AtomicResidualGraphAccepts q ∧ Relation.ReflTransGen AtomicResidualGraphStep q r ∧ Relation.ReflTransGen AtomicResidualGraphStep q t ∧ (¬ ∃ s : AtomicResidualState, AtomicResidualGraphStep r s) ∧ (¬ ∃ s : AtomicResidualState, AtomicResidualGraphStep t s) ∧ AtomicResidualGraphAccepts r ∧ ¬ AtomicResidualGraphAccepts t
priority_rationale: A one-off mixed-terminal example adds little beyond existing boundary evidence.

stmt: ¬ ∀ q : AtomicResidualState, AtomicResidualGraphAccepts q ↔ ∀ r : AtomicResidualState, Relation.ReflTransGen AtomicResidualGraphStep q r → (¬ ∃ s : AtomicResidualState, AtomicResidualGraphStep r s) → AtomicResidualGraphAccepts r
priority_rationale: Negative equivalence around terminal descendants is already structurally settled enough.

stmt: ¬ ∀ q : AtomicResidualState, AtomicResidualGraphAccepts q → ∃! r : AtomicResidualState, Relation.ReflTransGen AtomicResidualGraphStep q r ∧ (¬ ∃ s : AtomicResidualState, AtomicResidualGraphStep r s)
priority_rationale: Unique-terminal-sink failure is a local boundary example, not a current bottleneck.

stmt: ∃ q r : AtomicResidualState, AtomicResidualGraphAccepts q ∧ Relation.ReflTransGen AtomicResidualGraphStep q r ∧ ((∀ (L Δ Λ : List Tp) (A B : Tp), Cand.rdiv L B A Δ Λ ∈ candidates r.1 → ¬ (Δ ⇒ A)) ∧ ∀ (Γ₁ Δ R : List Tp) (A B : Tp), Cand.ldiv Γ₁ Δ A B R ∈ candidates r.1 → ¬ (Δ ⇒ A)) ∧ ¬ AtomicResidualGraphAccepts r
priority_rationale: Another crafted counterexample about blocked candidates is too local and negative.

stmt: ∀ t : String, ∃ q r : AtomicResidualState, q.2 = t ∧ r.2 = t ∧ AtomicResidualGraphAccepts q ∧ Relation.ReflTransGen AtomicResidualGraphStep q r ∧ (¬ ∃ s : AtomicResidualState, AtomicResidualGraphStep r s) ∧ ¬ AtomicResidualGraphAccepts r
summary_delta: The verified witness is built from a schematic one-atom pattern, so the next strong step is to show the mixed-terminal phenomenon is uniform in the atom label rather than a single ad hoc example. That would make the failure of the universal terminal-base picture structural across the theory.
priority_rationale: Uniformizing the mixed-terminal witness across atoms is still a one-family negative result.

stmt: For every `p : AtomicResidualState` and `R : Finset AtomicResidualState` with `q ∈ R ↔ Relation.ReflTransGen AtomicResidualGraphStep p q`, if `L := R.toList.mergeSort (fun a b => list_degree a.1 ≤ list_degree b.1)`, then there exists a unique `good : AtomicResidualState → Bool` such that `good q = false` for `q ∉ R`, and for every `q ∈ R`, `good q = true` iff `q = ([#(p.2)], p.2)` or there exists `r ∈ R` with `AtomicResidualGraphStep q r`, `List.idxOf r L < List.idxOf q L`, and `good r = true`.
summary_delta: This removes the extra acceptance clause and asks whether the backward recursion itself already determines the canonical label on the exact reachable DAG. That is a genuine strengthening of the solved result and gives the clean fixed-point characterization needed for reusable packaging.
priority_rationale: Already subsumed by the verified degree-sorted unique-labeling theorem.

stmt: ∀ p : AtomicResidualState, ¬ AtomicResidualGraphAccepts p ↔ ∀ r : AtomicResidualState, Relation.ReflTransGen AtomicResidualGraphStep p r → (¬ ∃ s : AtomicResidualState, AtomicResidualGraphStep r s) → r ≠ ([# (r.2)], r.2)
summary_delta: The solved terminal classification supplies the missing universal half of the frontier picture: rejection should mean that every reachable terminal state is non-base. This is not a cosmetic reformulation of the solved theorem, because it upgrades a pointwise terminal fact into a full reachable-region boundary criterion and would complete the intended asymmetric summary of acceptance versus rejection.
priority_rationale: This terminal-frontier rejection criterion is close to existing results and not a main gap.

stmt: For every `p : AtomicResidualState` and exact reachable finset `R`, the subset `{q ∈ R | AtomicResidualGraphAccepts q}` is the least subset `S ⊆ R` such that `([# (p.2)], p.2) ∈ S` and whenever `q ∈ R` has a one-step successor `r ∈ S`, one has `q ∈ S`.
summary_delta: This extracts the order-free structural content exposed by the solved reverse-sweep theorem: acceptance on the reachable DAG is the intrinsic backward closure of the distinguished base sink. That is not just a cosmetic restatement, because it removes dependence on a particular list order and yields a reusable fixed-point characterization for later order-independence and terminal-frontier arguments.
priority_rationale: Already subsumed by the verified least accepting reachable subset theorem.

stmt: ∀ p : AtomicResidualState, AtomicResidualGraphAccepts p ↔ ∃! r : AtomicResidualState, Relation.ReflTransGen AtomicResidualGraphStep p r ∧ (¬ ∃ s : AtomicResidualState, AtomicResidualGraphStep r s) ∧ AtomicResidualGraphAccepts r
summary_delta: The current theorem gives existence of an accepting terminal witness; the natural sharpened follow-up is uniqueness. That would expose the reachable terminal frontier as having at most one accepting sink, clarifying the global shape of acceptance in the residual DAG and giving a cleaner target for the planned executable labeling theorems.
priority_rationale: Accepting-terminal uniqueness is essentially forced by the terminal singleton-base theorem.

stmt: ∀ p : AtomicResidualState, ∃ r : AtomicResidualState, Relation.ReflTransGen AtomicResidualGraphStep p r ∧ (¬ ∃ s : AtomicResidualState, AtomicResidualGraphStep r s)
summary_delta: The solved acceptance theorem now complements the existing rejecting-terminal result, so the next reusable structural step is to show that every reachable residual region has at least one terminal state. This removes the current case split between accepting and rejecting roots and gives the unconditional sink-existence fact needed for any exact reachable-region reverse sweep or terminal-frontier package.
priority_rationale: Unconditional sink existence is plausible but no longer summary-changing.

stmt: For every `p : AtomicResidualState` and exact reachable finset `R`, if `L := R.toList.mergeSort (fun a b => list_degree a.1 ≤ list_degree b.1)`, then the label function obtained by an explicit `List.foldr` over `L` that starts from the all-`false` function and updates each `q` to `true` exactly when `q = ([# (p.2)], p.2)` or some `AtomicResidualGraphStep`-successor of `q` is already labeled `true` agrees with `AtomicResidualGraphAccepts` on every `q ∈ R`.
summary_delta: The solved theorem still packages the reverse sweep through an abstract unique-existence statement. An explicit fold-correctness theorem is the missing operational bridge from canonicity to a concrete executable recognizer on the exact reachable region, which is the main remaining bottleneck in the current theory summary.
priority_rationale: Concrete foldr correctness on the canonical degree-sorted order is a credible bridge to the arbitrary-order algorithm.

stmt: For every root state `p`, every finite set `R` with `q ∈ R ↔ Relation.ReflTransGen AtomicResidualGraphStep p q`, and every reverse-topological list `L` enumerating `R`, if one runs the usual right-to-left Boolean sweep on `L`, then after processing any suffix `U` of `L` the current true states are exactly the least subset of `U` containing `([# (p.2)], p.2)` when that state lies in `U` and closed under predecessors inside `U`.
summary_delta: This is the missing invariant that genuinely fuses the new least-subset packaging with the arbitrary-order sweep agenda. It is stronger and more reusable than only the terminal fold result, and it directly attacks the presentation-independence bottleneck highlighted in the current theory state.
priority_rationale: This is the missing reusable partial-sweep invariant for arbitrary reverse-topological computation.

stmt: For every root state `p`, every finite set `R` with `q ∈ R ↔ Relation.ReflTransGen AtomicResidualGraphStep p q`, and every reverse-topological list `L` enumerating `R`, the explicit `List.foldr` reverse sweep over `L` starting from the all-`false` table computes a Boolean table `good` such that `good q = true ↔ AtomicResidualGraphAccepts q` for every `q ∈ R`.
summary_delta: The solved result removes dependence on a special degree-sorted presentation at the specification level but still packages the scan existentially. Making the sweep explicit for arbitrary reverse-topological enumerations is the clean executable bridge the current theory still lacks.
priority_rationale: This is the direct explicit fold theorem that would make the reachable recognizer executable.

stmt: A lemma that suffixes of a reverse-topological enumeration remain reverse-topological for the induced processed subgraph.
summary_delta: Missing lemma needed for `paper_claim_reverse_topo_suffix_invariant`.
priority_rationale: Routine structural support for suffix processing with little standalone summary impact.

stmt: A one-step fold update lemma identifying the new marked set after adding the next state on the left.
summary_delta: Missing lemma needed for `paper_claim_reverse_topo_suffix_invariant`.
priority_rationale: A local fold-update lemma is useful scaffolding, but it is too proof-specific to change the frontier.

stmt: A coercion/transport lemma between Boolean markings on a suffix enumeration and membership in the induced predecessor-closed set.
summary_delta: Missing lemma needed for `paper_claim_reverse_topo_suffix_invariant`.
priority_rationale: Transport between Boolean tables and set membership is bookkeeping around the main sweep argument.

stmt: For every context Gamma and goal A, SupportClosure Gamma A holds if and only if support_ok Gamma A holds.
summary_delta: AutomatedTheoryConstruction/Scratch.lean:1:0: error: import AutomatedTheoryConstruction.Derived failed, environment already contains 'AutomatedTheoryConstruction.residualAtomicState.match_1' from AutomatedTheoryConstruction.Generated.C0005_support_handed_closure
priority_rationale: Already exactly covered by the verified support-closure equivalence; the remaining issue is only environment noise.

stmt: A paper-level explanation that support_ok is the extensional content of plain support closure.
summary_delta: Missing lemma needed for `paper_claim_support_closure_iff_support_ok`.
priority_rationale: This is an expository restatement of an already verified equivalence.
